other_observers: Renee Haip (my mom); Yes, I am the original finder of the bird, but was not sure if it was a juvenile Harlan's or Krider's until I got a determination from hawk experts.
specific_location: Weld County Road 32.5 about 1/4 mile west of the South Platte River. The closest house address was 10434 Weld County Road 32.5
description: I immediately recognized this bird as an unusual, pale juvenile Red-tailed Hawk. There were two other Red-tailed Hawks soaring in the vicinity with the same relative size and shape, but not coloration.  Dorsal surface: The Krider's back and wing was brown with buffy mottling overall, with light patches on the back and upper wing coverts. There were very bright wing panels. The tail is fully barred, but fades as it nears the base. Overall, the head is very light for a Red-tailed Hawk. It had a white crown with light brown marks, light brown marks on the cheeks, and a clear white throat.  Ventral surface: The Krider's had faint, but noticeable patagial bars and comas on the under wing, which means it is a Red-tailed Hawk. It was very pale underneath with a lightly dark-brown spotted belly band. The wings were brownish-black tipped and had brown with buffy edges on the trailing edge of the wing. The tail was white at the base and was buffy with dark brown barring. The tail is fainter in color towards the distal edges. It had yellow legs.Behaviors: This bird was riding thermals with prey in its talons. Along with the prey, it had picked up vegetation, which was still clenched in its talons and trailing behind. Two other typical Red-tailed Hawks and two adult Bald Eagles were also soaring in the area. The Krider's Red-tailed Hawk landed in a medium-sized tree on a branch that was swaying in the wind just on the other side of the road behind a fence. It landed on the backside of this tree where I could not clearly view any more behavior.Call: Did not vocalize.Plumage: Juvenile
photographer: Photographer: Joel Such, Joel Such, Joel Such
